macro_segments.title: The Principle of Non-Kosher Animal Products and the Camel Repetition in Torah

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the Principle: Non-Kosher Animals Produce Non-Kosher Offspring and Products
Gemara's Challenge to the Derivation from Repeated 'Gamal'
Why the Lists Were Repeated in Deuteronomy: The Shasua and Ra'ah/Da'ah Distinctions
The Rule: We Derive Lessons When Possible, Otherwise Attribute Repetition to Including New Species

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Shimon's Position on Appearance and the Status of Mixed-Species Offspring

micro_segments.title: Mishna: Sheep Giving Birth to Goat-like Offspring and Vice Versa—Exempt vs. Obligated in Firstborn
Rabbi Shimon's Stringent Standard: Head and Majority Must Resemble Mother
The Question: Does Appearance Determine Kashrut or Only Firstborn Status?
Inference from the Baraita: Head and Majority Required for Both Eating and Firstborn
Counter-argument: The Baraita Jumped Over Eating and Only Mentioned Firstborn
Resolution: The Baraita Had to Teach Head and Majority for Firstborn to Correct a Misconception

macro_segments.title: The Requirement of One Characteristic Like the Mother to Render Mixed-Species Offspring Kosher

micro_segments.title: Baraita: Non-Kosher-Looking Offspring with Even One Maternal Characteristic Remains Kosher
Clarification: Siman Echad Means Physical Characteristic, Not Necessarily Kosher Trait
Practical Application: Even Defective Offspring from Kosher Parents Can Be Kosher

macro_segments.title: The Role of Paternity and Rabbi Yehoshua vs. Rabbi Eliezer on Parent Requirements

micro_segments.title: Baraita: Definition of Mixed-Species Offspring—Both Parents Must Be Kosher According to Rabbi Yehoshua
Verse Analysis: The Doubled 'Seh' Suggests Maternal Determination for Rabbi Yehoshua
Rabbi Eliezer's Position: Both Parents Being Kosher Makes Offspring Obviously Kosher
Rabbi Eliezer's Exception: Even Non-Kosher Father's Offspring Can Be Kosher

macro_segments.title: The Biological Feasibility of Kosher-Non-Kosher Animal Mating and Rabbi Yirmiyah's Resolution

micro_segments.title: Gemara's Challenge: How Can Kosher and Non-Kosher Animals Mate If No Natural Interbreeding Occurs?
Rabbi Yehoshua ben Levi's Rule: No Cross-Species Breeding Among Kosher-Non-Kosher, Large-Small, or Domestic-Wild
Exception: The Disputed Status of the Koy as Possible Hybrid of Wild and Domestic
Rabbi Yirmiyah's Resolution: The 'Non-Kosher' Parent is Actually a Genetically Kosher Animal Appearing Non-Kosher
Implication: All Positions Agree That Appearance Matters; Disagreement Only on Degree Required

macro_segments.title: Two Competing Views on What Constitutes Dual Causation and Parentage in Determining Offspring Status

micro_segments.title: General Rule: Both Mother and Father as Causes—Rabbi Eliezer Says One Problematic Parent Forbids Offspring
Exceptional Case of Kosher Animals: Only Mother's Species Determines Kosher Status
Reverse Case of Sacred Animals: Torah Requires Both Parents to Be Kosher, Suggesting Both Matter
Gemara's Explanation: Different Cases Reverse Their Positions, Proving Neither Has Absolute Rule

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Shimon's Explicit Standard: Majority and Head Determine Kashrut of Mixed-Appearance Offspring

micro_segments.title: Baraita: Rabbi Shimon Requires Head and Majority Resemblance to Mother for Eating Permit
Clear Inference: Rabbi Shimon's Standard Applies to Both Eating and Firstborn Status

macro_segments.title: Products of Non-Kosher Animals: Milk, Urine, and the Definition of What Constitutes a 'Product'

micro_segments.title: Question About Donkey Urine: Is It Considered a Product or Just Water Passing Through?
Horses and Camels Excluded: Urine is Clear and Resembles Water, Not Considered Food
Donkey Urine Question: It Is Foul and Cloudy, Resembling Milk—Is It a True Product?
Rav Sheshet's Answer: The Principle Teaches Products of Non-Kosher Are Non-Kosher
Alternative View: The Question Was Only About Donkey Urine Because People Actually Drink It
Conclusion: Donkey Urine Is Forbidden Because It Is a Product of a Non-Kosher Animal

macro_segments.title: Bee Honey as an Exception: Is It Considered a Product or Not Transformed by the Bee?

micro_segments.title: Gemara's Question: Why Is Bee Honey Permitted If It Comes from a Non-Kosher Creature?
Standard Answer: Bees Store Nectar in a Non-Digestive Stomach and Do Not Transform It
Two Interpretations of Honey Production: Storage vs. External Transformation
Rabbi Yaakov's Position: Honey Is Fundamentally Forbidden but Given Special Torah Permission
Derivation: From the Phrase 'You Shall Not Eat of Crawling Things'—Bee Honey Is the Sole Exception
Limiting the Exception: Bee Honey Only, Not Hornet or Wasp Honey, Because It Needs No Qualifier
Alternative View: Honey of Wasps and Hornets Is Also Permitted Because It Is Not Considered Produced
Implication: The Disagreement Hinges on Whether Honey-Making Is a True Internal Transformation

macro_segments.title: Placenta and Other Animal Byproducts: Refuse vs. Product Status

micro_segments.title: Mysterious Egg-Like Substances from Donkey Wombs: Possibly Kidney Stones or Other Calculi
Two Interpretations: Torn-Off Testicles vs. Dried Semen from a Kosher Animal Mate
Placenta: Permitted as Refuse Without Tumah Status Regardless of Mother's Life or Death
Conceptual Issue: Why Placenta as Refuse but Not Urine—What Defines Refuse vs. Product?

topical_tags.term: יוצא מן הטמא טמא (Yotzei Min Ha-Tamei Tamei)
Paternity/Father's Role (זרע האב / חוששים לזרע האב)
Kolut (קלוט) - Animal Looking Like Different Species
Is Honey a Product or Stored (לא ממצות אותן מגופן)
Gemara's Challenge to Derivations (אלא מעתה / מיתיבי)
